\documentclass[11pt]{article}
\usepackage[margin=1in]{geometry}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}
\usepackage{booktabs}
\usepackage{hyperref}
\usepackage{cleveref}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{lemma}[theorem]{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}[theorem]{Proposition}
\newtheorem{definition}[theorem]{Definition}
\newtheorem{remark}[theorem]{Remark}
\newtheorem{corollary}[theorem]{Corollary}
\newtheorem{example}[theorem]{Example}

\DeclareMathOperator{\tr}{tr}
\DeclareMathOperator{\diag}{diag}
\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\Prob}{\mathbb{P}}
\newcommand{\calP}{\mathcal{P}}
\newcommand{\calU}{\mathcal{U}}
\newcommand{\calM}{\mathcal{M}}
\newcommand{\calC}{\mathcal{C}}

\title{Distributionally Robust Taint Reachability\\
for Agentic Information Flow Control}
\author{Technical Reference}
\date{\today}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
We consider the problem of bounding the probability that sensitive data
reaches an unsafe channel after an agent executes a sequence of shell
commands. The taint state evolves under \emph{deterministic} Boolean
transitions (copy, merge, delete) and \emph{stochastic} declassification
actions that succeed with a known probability~$\gamma$ and fail with
probability~$1-\gamma$. The initial taint state is uncertain: a PII
detector provides marginal confidence scores
$\ell_i \le \E[s_i[0]] \le u_i$ for each file, but we make \emph{no assumptions}
about the joint correlations of these scores. We formalize the
worst-case taint reachability as a distributionally robust optimization
(DRO) problem and show it admits a polynomial-time SDP relaxation via
degree-2 sum-of-squares (SOS) certificates. We also present a
\emph{spectral relaxation} based on generalized eigenvalue problems
that replaces the SDP with an eigenvalue computation, offering
adaptive risk bounds at a fraction of the cost.
\end{abstract}


%% ========================================================================
\section{Problem Setup}
%% ========================================================================

\subsection{State Variables}

An agentic system operates on $N$ data objects (files, variables,
channels). Each object $i$ has a Boolean taint indicator:
\[
  s_i[t] \in \{0, 1\}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, N, \quad t = 0, 1, \ldots, T,
\]
where $s_i[t] = 1$ means object $i$ is tainted (contains or is derived
from sensitive data) at time $t$.

\subsection{Initial Conditions (Marginal Bounds Only)}

A PII detector classifies each object as sensitive or clean with
some confidence. This yields an \emph{interval bound} on each
marginal:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:initial}
  \ell_i \;\le\; \E[s_i[0]] \;\le\; u_i \qquad \forall i = 1, \ldots, N,
\end{equation}
where:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Classified sensitive} with confidence $c$:
        $\ell_i = c$, $u_i = 1$.
        \\(e.g., detector says ``PII detected'' at 90\%
        $\Rightarrow m_i \ge 0.9$.)
  \item \textbf{Classified clean} with confidence $c$:
        $\ell_i = 0$, $u_i = 1 - c$.
        \\(e.g., detector says ``no PII'' at 80\%
        $\Rightarrow m_i \le 0.2$.)
\end{itemize}
Crucially, we make \textbf{no assumptions} about the joint distribution
of $(s_1[0], \ldots, s_N[0])$. The detector might use a shared model,
the files might share content, or the detections might be arbitrarily
correlated---we do not know and do not assume.

\begin{definition}[Ambiguity Set]
The set of all initial distributions consistent with the marginal
interval bounds is:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:ambiguity}
  \calP = \left\{
    \mu \in \Delta(\{0,1\}^N) :
    \ell_i \le \E_\mu[s_i[0]] \le u_i \;\;\forall i
  \right\}.
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\subsection{Transition Model}

At each time step, the agent issues a command $a[t]$ drawn from a
finite command vocabulary. The state evolves as:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:transition}
  s[t+1] = F_{a[t]}(s[t], \omega[t]),
\end{equation}
where $\omega[t]$ is an independent noise source used \emph{only}
by declassification actions. Commands fall into two categories:

\subsubsection{Deterministic Transitions}

Most commands implement a deterministic Boolean function:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
\textbf{Command} & \textbf{Semantics} & \textbf{Example} \\
\midrule
\textsc{Propagate} & $s_\text{dst}' = s_\text{src}$
  & \texttt{cp src dst} \\
\textsc{Merge} & $s_\text{dst}' = s_1 \vee s_2$
  & \texttt{cat f1 f2 > out} \\
\textsc{Delete} & $s_\text{target}' = 0$
  & \texttt{rm file} \\
\textsc{CreateClean} & $s_\text{dst}' = 0$
  & \texttt{echo "hello" > file} \\
\textsc{CreateTainted} & $s_\text{dst}' = 1$
  & (unknown tool output) \\
\textsc{Identity} & $s' = s$
  & \texttt{ls}, \texttt{read\_file} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

For deterministic transitions, $\omega[t]$ is unused.

\subsubsection{Stochastic Declassification}

A declassification action on object $i$ with confidence $\gamma \in [0,1]$:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:declassify}
  s_i[t+1] = s_i[t] \cdot (1 - \omega[t]),
  \qquad \omega[t] \sim \text{Ber}(\gamma).
\end{equation}
When $\omega = 1$ (probability $\gamma$), the file is successfully
sanitized. When $\omega = 0$, the taint persists. We make
\textbf{no assumption} about the dependence between $\omega[t]$
and the state $s[t]$; the cross-moment $\E[s_i \omega]$ is a free
variable subject to Fr\'echet bounds, just as with \textsc{Merge}.
All other variables are unchanged.

\subsubsection{Contract-Based Modeling of Arbitrary Commands}

The commands above cover standard POSIX operations. For arbitrary
agent tools (API calls, custom scripts, LLM-generated functions),
we assign taint semantics via a \emph{state propagation contract}:

\begin{definition}[State Propagation Contract]
A contract $\calC = (\texttt{inputs}, \texttt{outputs},
\texttt{mode}, \gamma)$ declares:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\texttt{inputs} \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$: objects read,
  \item $\texttt{outputs} \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$: objects written,
  \item $\texttt{mode} \in \calM$: one of the transition types above
        (\textsc{Propagate}, \textsc{Merge}, \textsc{Delete},
        \textsc{Declassify}, \textsc{Identity}, \textsc{CreateClean},
        \textsc{CreateTainted}),
  \item $\gamma \in [0, 1]$: confidence that the tool implements the
        declared mode correctly.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

The contract maps an unknown tool to a known transition type.
For example:
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{llll}
\toprule
\textbf{Tool} & \textbf{Mode} & \textbf{$\gamma$}
  & \textbf{Effect} \\
\midrule
\texttt{redact\_pii(f)} & \textsc{Declassify} & 0.95
  & $m_f' \le \min(m_f,\, 0.05)$ \\
\texttt{sha256sum(f)} & \textsc{Declassify} & 1.0
  & $m_f' = 0$ \\
\texttt{translate(src, dst)} & \textsc{Propagate} & 1.0
  & $m_\text{dst}' = m_\text{src}$ \\
\texttt{summarize(f1, f2, out)} & \textsc{Merge} & 1.0
  & $m_\text{out}' = m_1 + m_2 - m_{12}$ \\
\texttt{fetch\_url(url, dst)} & \textsc{CreateTainted} & 1.0
  & $m_\text{dst}' = 1$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

When $\gamma < 1$, the transition is a convex combination of the
declared semantics and the worst case (\textsc{CreateTainted} on all
outputs):
\begin{equation}\label{eq:confidence}
  M_c^{(\gamma)} = \gamma \cdot M_c + (1 - \gamma) \cdot
  M_\textsc{CreateTainted}.
\end{equation}

\begin{remark}[Uncontracted Commands]
Any command without a contract is treated conservatively as
\textsc{CreateTainted} on all arguments appearing in output
position (i.e., $s_\text{out}' = 1$). This ensures the framework
is sound even for commands it has never seen.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Unsafe Set and Objective}

Given a set of unsafe variables $\calU \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$
(e.g., outbound network channels), the \emph{leakage probability} is:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:objective}
  R^* = \sup_{\mu \in \calP} \;\;
  \E_\mu\!\left[\max_{i \in \calU}\, s_i[T]\right]
\end{equation}
subject to the transition dynamics \eqref{eq:transition}.

Since each $s_i \in \{0,1\}$, we have
$\max_{i \in \calU} s_i = \mathbf{1}[\exists i \in \calU : s_i = 1]$,
so:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:max-is-prob}
  \E\!\left[\max_{i \in \calU}\, s_i[T]\right]
  = \Prob\!\left(\bigvee_{i \in \calU} s_i[T] = 1\right)
  = \Prob(\textsc{Leak}).
\end{equation}
Thus $R^*$ is the worst-case probability that \emph{any} tainted
object reaches an unsafe channel, maximized over all initial
distributions consistent with the marginal bounds.

\begin{remark}[Exact vs.\ Relaxed]
For $|\calU| \le 2$, $\Prob(\textsc{Leak})$ is exactly representable
in terms of first and second moments (no relaxation needed):
\begin{align}
  |\calU| = 1 &: \quad \Prob(\textsc{Leak}) = m_u[T], \\
  |\calU| = 2 &: \quad \Prob(\textsc{Leak}) = m_{u_1}[T] + m_{u_2}[T]
    - m_{u_1 u_2}[T].
\end{align}
For $|\calU| \ge 3$, the exact inclusion-exclusion involves higher-order
moments. We compute a tight upper bound via the SDP dual below.
\end{remark}

%% ========================================================================
\section{Propagating Expectations Through Transitions}
\label{sec:propagation}
%% ========================================================================

We now derive how $\E[s_i[t]]$ and $\E[s_i[t] s_j[t]]$ transform
under each command. Write
$m_i[t] = \E[s_i[t]]$ and
$m_{ij}[t] = \E[s_i[t]\, s_j[t]]$.

\subsection{Deterministic Commands}

\noindent\textbf{\textsc{Propagate}} ($s_\text{dst}' = s_\text{src}$):
\begin{align}
  m_\text{dst}' &= m_\text{src}, &
  m_{\text{dst},k}' &= m_{\text{src},k} \;\;\forall k \ne \text{dst}.
  \label{eq:prop-moments}
\end{align}

\noindent\textbf{\textsc{Merge}} ($s_\text{dst}' = s_1 \vee s_2$).\;
Since $s_1 \vee s_2 = s_1 + s_2 - s_1 s_2$ on $\{0,1\}$:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:merge-first}
  m_\text{dst}' = m_1 + m_2 - m_{12}.
\end{equation}
The cross-moment $m_{12}$ is constrained by Fr\'echet bounds
$\max(0, m_1 + m_2 - 1) \le m_{12} \le \min(m_1, m_2)$
but is otherwise \emph{free} in our model (no correlation assumptions).

For the worst-case (maximizing $m_\text{dst}'$), we take $m_{12}$ at
its lower bound:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:merge-worst}
  m_\text{dst}' \le m_1 + m_2 - \max(0, m_1 + m_2 - 1)
  = \min(m_1 + m_2,\; 1).
\end{equation}

\noindent\textbf{\textsc{Delete} / \textsc{CreateClean}}:
$m_\text{target}' = 0$, $m_{\text{target},k}' = 0$.

\noindent\textbf{\textsc{CreateTainted}}:
$m_\text{dst}' = 1$, $m_{\text{dst},k}' = m_k$.

\noindent\textbf{\textsc{Identity}}: all moments unchanged.

\subsection{Declassification (DRO over Noise)}

Declassification of object $i$ with confidence $\gamma$ gives
$s_i' = s_i (1 - \omega)$ where $\E[\omega] = \gamma$. We make no
assumption about how $\omega$ correlates with $s_i$. The
first moment is:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:declass-first}
  m_i' = \E[s_i(1 - \omega)] = m_i - \E[s_i \omega].
\end{equation}
The cross-moment $\E[s_i \omega]$ is subject to Fr\'echet bounds:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:declass-frechet}
  \max(0,\, m_i + \gamma - 1) \;\le\; \E[s_i \omega]
  \;\le\; \min(m_i,\, \gamma).
\end{equation}
For the worst case (maximizing $m_i'$), we minimize $\E[s_i \omega]$:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:declass-worst}
  m_i' \le m_i - \max(0,\, m_i + \gamma - 1)
  = \min(m_i,\; 1 - \gamma).
\end{equation}
This is the \emph{same Fr\'echet machinery} used for
\textsc{Merge}. The cross-moment with any other variable
$k \ne i$ transforms as:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:declass-cross}
  m_{i,k}' = \E[s_i s_k (1 - \omega)]
  = m_{ik} - \E[s_i s_k \omega],
\end{equation}
where $\E[s_i s_k \omega]$ is again a free cross-moment in the
DRO model, subject to its own Fr\'echet bounds.

Note: if one additionally assumes $\omega \perp\!\!\!\perp s$,
then $\E[s_i \omega] = m_i \gamma$ and $m_i' = (1-\gamma) m_i$,
which is always $\le \min(m_i, 1-\gamma)$. The DRO formulation
subsumes this as a special case while remaining sound without
the independence assumption.

%% ========================================================================
\section{The DRO Problem and Its SDP Relaxation}
\label{sec:dro}
%% ========================================================================

\subsection{Compositional State at Time $T$}

Given a fixed command sequence $a[0], \ldots, a[T-1]$, each
$s_i[T]$ is a (possibly random) function of the initial state
$s[0]$ and the declassification noise variables
$\omega[0], \ldots, \omega[T-1]$. Since the noise is independent,
we can take expectations over $\omega$ first:
\[
  \E[s_i[T]] = \E_\omega\!\left[\E_{s[0]}[s_i[T] \mid \omega]\right].
\]
Conditioned on $\omega$, the transitions are deterministic, so
$s_i[T \mid \omega]$ is a Boolean function of $s[0]$.

Concretely, composing the transition matrices:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:composed}
  \E[s_i[T]] = \mathbf{e}_i^\top
  \left(\prod_{t=T-1}^{0} M_{a[t]}\right) \mathbf{m}[0],
\end{equation}
where $M_{a[t]}$ is the (deterministic or scaled) moment
transition matrix and $\mathbf{m}[0] = (1, m_1[0], \ldots, m_N[0],
m_{12}[0], \ldots)^\top$ is the initial moment vector.

\subsection{Dual of the Moment Problem}

The DRO problem \eqref{eq:objective} asks to maximize
$\Prob(\textsc{Leak})$ over the ambiguity set. By LP duality
(the primal has $2^N$ variables $\mu(\mathbf{s})$, one per vertex),
the dual seeks a degree-$\le 2$ polynomial
$f(\mathbf{s}) = \mathbf{c}^\top \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{s})$
that dominates the leak indicator:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:dual-general}
  R^* \le \min_{\mathbf{c}} \;\; \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{b}
  \qquad \text{s.t.} \qquad
  f(\mathbf{s}) \ge \mathbf{1}_\textsc{Leak}(\mathbf{s})
  \;\;\forall \mathbf{s} \in \{0,1\}^N,
\end{equation}
where $\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{s}) = (1, s_1, \ldots, s_N,
s_1 s_2, \ldots)^\top$ is the moment basis and $\mathbf{b}$
encodes the marginal bounds.

\subsection{Decomposing the Leak Indicator}

The key observation: since $\mathbf{s} \in \{0,1\}^N$,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:max-simplex}
  \max_{i \in \calU} s_i
  = \sup_{\boldsymbol{\alpha} \in \Delta_{|\calU|}}
    \sum_{i \in \calU} \alpha_i s_i,
\end{equation}
where $\Delta_{|\calU|}$ is the simplex. Therefore the constraint
$f(\mathbf{s}) \ge \mathbf{1}_\textsc{Leak}(\mathbf{s})$
is \textbf{equivalent} to:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:decomposed}
  f(\mathbf{s}) \ge s_i \qquad \forall \mathbf{s} \in \{0,1\}^N,
  \quad \forall i \in \calU.
\end{equation}

\begin{proof}[Equivalence]
$(\Leftarrow)$: If any $s_i = 1$ ($i \in \calU$), then
$f(\mathbf{s}) \ge s_i = 1 = \mathbf{1}_\textsc{Leak}$.
If all $s_i = 0$, then $f(\mathbf{s}) \ge 0$ (from the non-negativity
certificate below). $(\Rightarrow)$: If $\mathbf{1}_\textsc{Leak} = 1$,
then $f \ge 1 \ge s_i$. If $\mathbf{1}_\textsc{Leak} = 0$, then
all $s_i = 0$, so $f \ge 0 = s_i$.
\end{proof}

Unlike the sum relaxation
$f(\mathbf{s}) \ge \sum_{i \in \calU} s_i$
(which is a \emph{single, strictly stronger} constraint), the
decomposition \eqref{eq:decomposed} uses $|\calU|$ \emph{weaker}
constraints---yielding a \emph{larger} feasible set and hence a
\emph{tighter} (smaller) upper bound on~$R^*$.

\subsection{SOS Certificates (Vertex-Free)}

Each constraint in \eqref{eq:decomposed} is a polynomial
non-negativity condition on $\{0,1\}^N$. We certify it with a
degree-2 SOS decomposition (\Cref{thm:sos-cert} below).

\begin{theorem}[Degree-2 SOS Certificate]
\label{thm:sos-cert}
A degree-$\le 2$ polynomial $p(\mathbf{s}) \ge 0$ on $\{0,1\}^N$
if there exists a PSD Gram matrix $Q \in \mathcal{S}^{N+1}_+$ such
that, writing $\hat{\mathbf{s}} = (1, s_1, \ldots, s_N)^\top$:
\[
  p(\mathbf{s}) = \hat{\mathbf{s}}^\top Q \, \hat{\mathbf{s}}
  + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \underbrace{Q_{1+i,1+i}}_{\lambda_i}
  \cdot s_i(1 - s_i).
\]
Matching polynomial coefficients eliminates the
$\lambda_i = Q_{1+i,1+i}$ and yields linear constraints on~$Q$.
\end{theorem}

\subsection{Complete SDP Formulation}

\begin{equation}\label{eq:sdp-tight}
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
  R^* \le \bar{R} = \min_{\mathbf{c},\, Q_0,\, Q_1, \ldots, Q_{|\calU|}}
  \;\; & \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{b} \\[3pt]
  \text{s.t.} \;\;
  & \textsc{CoeffMatch}\bigl(
      \mathbf{c}^\top \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{s}),\; Q_0\bigr)
    & & \text{(non-negativity)} \\
  & \textsc{CoeffMatch}\bigl(
      \mathbf{c}^\top \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{s}) - s_i,\; Q_i\bigr)
    \quad \forall i \in \calU
    & & \text{(leak detection)} \\
  & Q_0, Q_1, \ldots, Q_{|\calU|} \succeq 0
\end{aligned}
}
\end{equation}

Here $\mathbf{b}$ encodes the marginal bounds: for each coefficient
$c_j$, the corresponding entry $b_j$ is chosen to make
$\mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{b}$ an upper bound (i.e., $b_j = u_j$
when $c_j > 0$ via LP duality of the box constraints).

\begin{proposition}[Complexity]
The SDP \eqref{eq:sdp-tight} has $d = O(N^2)$ variables
($\mathbf{c}$), $|\calU| + 1$ PSD cones of size $(N+1)$, and
$O(|\calU| \cdot N^2)$ coefficient-matching linear constraints.
Interior-point solvers solve this in $O(|\calU| \cdot N^{3.5})$ time.
\end{proposition}

\begin{remark}[Comparison with Sum Relaxation]
Replacing \eqref{eq:decomposed} with the single constraint
$f(\mathbf{s}) \ge \sum_i s_i$ gives $\bar{R}_\text{sum} \ge \bar{R}$.
The gap can be up to a factor of $|\calU|$ (e.g., when all marginals
equal $p$ and $|\calU| p \ge 1$, the sum bound saturates at $1$ while
the tight bound is $\min(1, |\calU| p - \binom{|\calU|}{2} p^2 + \cdots)$).
\end{remark}

\subsection{When Cross-Moments Are Irrelevant}

For many common command sequences, the composed transition
$M_\text{comp}$ only depends on the first-order moments $m_i$, not
the cross-moments $m_{ij}$. This happens when:
\begin{itemize}
  \item All commands are \textsc{Propagate}, \textsc{Delete},
        \textsc{Identity}, or \textsc{Declassify} (all degree-1
        operations),
  \item No \textsc{Merge} operation appears in the sequence.
\end{itemize}

In this case, the SDP reduces to $N$ independent scalar problems:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:scalar}
  R^* = \max_{i \in \calU} [\text{coefficient}_i \cdot u_i],
\end{equation}
which is $O(N)$ and closed-form.

\subsection{The Effect of \textsc{Merge}}

\textsc{Merge} is the only deterministic command that introduces
dependence on cross-moments (because $s_1 \vee s_2 = s_1 + s_2 -
s_1 s_2$). When a merge appears, the LP must optimize over the
cross-moment $m_{12}$ subject to Fr\'echet bounds.

\begin{example}
Consider $N = 3$: files $A$, $B$, and \texttt{OUT} $\in \calU$.
The agent executes: \texttt{cat A B > OUT}.

The composed transition gives: $m_\text{OUT}[1] = m_A + m_B - m_{AB}$.

The DRO problem is:
\[
  R^* = \max_{\ell_A \le m_A \le u_A,\; \ell_B \le m_B \le u_B}
  \max_{\max(0, m_A + m_B - 1) \le m_{AB} \le \min(m_A, m_B)}
  m_A + m_B - m_{AB}.
\]
The inner max minimizes $m_{AB}$:
$m_{AB}^* = \max(0, m_A + m_B - 1)$, giving
$m_\text{OUT}^* = \min(m_A + m_B, 1)$.

The outer max sets $m_A = u_A$, $m_B = u_B$:
\[
  R^* = \min(u_A + u_B, 1).
\]
This is exactly the union bound, and it is \emph{tight}---there
exists a joint distribution achieving it (maximally anti-correlated).
\end{example}

\begin{example}
Now add a declassification: \texttt{redact A} ($\gamma = 0.9$),
then \texttt{cat A B > OUT}.

By~\eqref{eq:declass-worst}: $m_A' \le \min(m_A,\, 0.1)$.

After merge: $m_\text{OUT} = m_A' + m_B - m_{A'B}
\le \min(m_A' + m_B,\, 1)$.

The DRO bound optimizes over both $m_A' \le \min(u_A, 0.1)$
and the merge cross-moment. Since $u_A = 0.8$, we have
$m_A' \le 0.1$ and:
\[
  R^* = \min(0.1 + u_B, 1).
\]
With $u_B = 0.6$: $R^* = 0.7$.
Compare with the independence-based bound: $\min(0.1 u_A + u_B, 1)
= \min(0.08 + 0.6, 1) = 0.68$. The DRO bound is slightly more
conservative but requires no modeling assumption on the
declassification noise.
\end{example}

%% ========================================================================
\section{SDP Strengthening (Moment Matrix Relaxation)}
\label{sec:sdp}
%% ========================================================================

The LP \eqref{eq:dual-general} uses only the Fr\'echet bounds on
cross-moments. A tighter relaxation uses the \emph{moment matrix}:

\begin{definition}[Moment Matrix]
The $(N+1) \times (N+1)$ moment matrix is:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:moment-matrix}
  \mathbf{Y} = \E\!\left[
    \hat{\mathbf{s}} \hat{\mathbf{s}}^\top
  \right], \qquad
  \hat{\mathbf{s}} = (1, s_1, \ldots, s_N)^\top.
\end{equation}
A necessary condition for $\mathbf{Y}$ to correspond to a valid
distribution is $\mathbf{Y} \succeq 0$.
\end{definition}

Adding $\mathbf{Y} \succeq 0$ to \eqref{eq:dual-general} yields an
SDP that is strictly tighter than the LP: it excludes moment vectors
that satisfy Fr\'echet bounds individually but cannot arise from any
joint distribution.

\begin{equation}\label{eq:sdp}
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
  R^* \le \bar{R}_\text{SDP} = \max_{\mathbf{m}[0],\, \mathbf{Y}} \quad
  & \sum_{i \in \calU}
    \mathbf{e}_i^\top M_\text{comp} \, \mathbf{m}[0] \\
  \text{s.t.} \quad
  & \ell_i \le m_i \le u_i \quad \forall i \\
  & Y_{0,0} = 1, \; Y_{0,i} = m_i, \; Y_{i,j} = m_{ij} \\
  & Y_{i,i} = m_i \quad \forall i
    & & (s_i^2 = s_i) \\
  & \mathbf{Y} \succeq 0
\end{aligned}
}
\end{equation}

\begin{proposition}
For $N \le 2$, the LP and SDP bounds coincide. For $N \ge 3$, the
SDP can be strictly tighter (it captures constraints from the PSD
condition that the pairwise Fr\'echet bounds miss).
\end{proposition}

%% ========================================================================
\section{Online Shield Protocol}
\label{sec:shield}
%% ========================================================================

\noindent\textbf{Algorithm: Distributionally Robust Taint Shield}
\begin{quote}
\textbf{Input:} Variables $\{1, \ldots, N\}$, unsafe set $\calU$,
tolerance $\varepsilon$ \\[2pt]
$\mathbf{m}[0] \gets (\ell_1, u_1, \ldots, \ell_N, u_N)$ from PII detector \\[2pt]
\textbf{for each} proposed command $a_t$:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Compile transition matrix $M_{a_t}$ from contract
  \item Compute tentative next moments
        $\mathbf{m}' = M_{a_t} \, \mathbf{m}[t]$
  \item Solve the SDP \eqref{eq:sdp-tight}
        to obtain $\bar{R}$
  \item \textbf{if} $\bar{R} \le \varepsilon$:
        \textsc{Allow}, commit $\mathbf{m}[t+1] = \mathbf{m}'$
  \item \textbf{else}: \textsc{Block}, suggest safe fallback
        (e.g., declassify the offending source)
\end{enumerate}
\end{quote}

\begin{theorem}[Soundness]
\label{thm:soundness}
If the shield allows a command sequence,
then for \emph{every} initial distribution $\mu \in \calP$
and \emph{every} realization of the declassification noise:
\[
  \Prob_\mu(\textsc{Leak})
  = \E_\mu\!\left[\max_{i \in \calU}\, s_i[T]\right]
  \le \bar{R} \le \varepsilon.
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The shield allows only when $\bar{R} \le \varepsilon$.
By LP duality of the moment problem (strong duality holds since the
primal is feasible and bounded), $\bar{R} \ge R^*$.
Since $R^* = \sup_\mu \Prob_\mu(\textsc{Leak})$, we have
$\Prob_\mu(\textsc{Leak}) \le R^* \le \bar{R} \le \varepsilon$ for all
$\mu \in \calP$.
\end{proof}

%% ========================================================================
\section{Online Spectral Relaxation}
\label{sec:spectral}
%% ========================================================================

The online SDP (\Cref{sec:shield}) adapts to the current state but
requires an interior-point solver at $O(|\calU| \cdot N^{3.5})$ per
command. We now present a \emph{spectral relaxation} that also adapts
to the current state but replaces the SDP solver with eigenvalue
computations, at the cost of a potentially more conservative bound.

\subsection{Block-Diagonal Gram Matrices}

Consider a degree-$\le 2$ polynomial
$p(\mathbf{s}) = \alpha_0 + \sum_{j=1}^N \alpha_j s_j + \sum_{1 \le j < k \le N} \alpha_{jk} s_j s_k$
and the SOS certificate from \Cref{thm:sos-cert}:
\[
  p(\mathbf{s}) = \hat{\mathbf{s}}^\top Q \, \hat{\mathbf{s}}
  + \sum_{i=1}^N Q_{1+i,1+i} \cdot s_i(1 - s_i),
  \qquad Q \in \mathcal{S}^{N+1}_+.
\]
The coefficient-matching conditions
(\Cref{thm:sos-cert}) relate $Q$ to $p$ as:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:coeff-match-explicit}
  Q_{0,0} = \alpha_0, \qquad
  2 Q_{0,j} + Q_{j,j} = \alpha_j \;\;\forall j \ge 1, \qquad
  2 Q_{j,k} = \alpha_{jk} \;\;\forall 1 \le j < k \le N.
\end{equation}
The off-diagonal entries $Q_{j,k}$ ($j,k \ge 1$, $j \ne k$)
are determined, but the {\bfseries diagonal entries
$Q_{j,j}$ ($j \ge 1$) are free parameters}, with $Q_{0,j}$
then fixed by $Q_{0,j} = (\alpha_j - Q_{j,j})/2$.

\begin{definition}[Canonical Block-Diagonal Choice]
\label{def:canonical-gram}
Set $Q_{j,j} = \alpha_j$ for all $j \ge 1$. Then $Q_{0,j} = 0$
for all $j \ge 1$, and $Q$ becomes \textbf{block-diagonal}:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:block-gram}
  Q = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_0 & \mathbf{0}^\top \\ \mathbf{0} & A(p) \end{pmatrix},
\end{equation}
where $A(p) \in \R^{N \times N}$ is the symmetric matrix:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Ap-def}
  A(p)_{j,j} = \alpha_j, \qquad
  A(p)_{j,k} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\alpha_{jk} \quad (j \ne k).
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Correctness of the canonical choice]
\label{lem:canonical-correct}
Writing $\hat{\mathbf{s}} = (1, s_1, \ldots, s_N)^\top$ and $Q$ as
in~\eqref{eq:block-gram}:
\[
  \hat{\mathbf{s}}^\top Q \, \hat{\mathbf{s}}
  = \alpha_0 + \sum_j \alpha_j s_j^2 + \sum_{j<k} \alpha_{jk} s_j s_k.
\]
Adding the correction terms $\sum_j Q_{j,j} \cdot s_j(1-s_j)
= \sum_j \alpha_j (s_j - s_j^2)$ gives
$p(\mathbf{s}) = \alpha_0 + \sum_j \alpha_j s_j
+ \sum_{j<k} \alpha_{jk} s_j s_k$. \qed
\end{lemma}

\begin{proposition}[Spectral Certificate]
\label{prop:spectral-cert}
$Q \succeq 0$ if and only if $\alpha_0 \ge 0$ and $A(p) \succeq 0$
(by the block-diagonal structure~\eqref{eq:block-gram}).
Therefore:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:spectral-cert}
  \alpha_0 \ge 0 \;\;\text{and}\;\;
  \lambda_{\min}\!\bigl(A(p)\bigr) \ge 0
  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
  p(\mathbf{s}) \ge 0 \;\;\forall\, \mathbf{s} \in \{0,1\}^N.
\end{equation}
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
A block-diagonal matrix $\diag(\alpha_0, A(p))$ is PSD iff each block
is PSD. The scalar $\alpha_0 \ge 0$ is its own block. By
\Cref{thm:sos-cert}, $Q \succeq 0$ certifies $p \ge 0$ on
$\{0,1\}^N$.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Sufficient but not necessary]
The implication~\eqref{eq:spectral-cert} is \textbf{sufficient but not
necessary}: the full SOS SDP~\eqref{eq:sdp-tight} optimizes over all
$Q_{j,j} \ge 0$, whereas the canonical choice fixes $Q_{j,j} = \alpha_j$.
This restricts the Gram matrix to a single point in the feasible set,
potentially rejecting polynomials that would pass the full SOS test
with a different diagonal.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Explicit Spectral Formulation for the DRO Problem}

We now apply \Cref{prop:spectral-cert} to each constraint
in~\eqref{eq:sdp-tight}. Write the certificate polynomial as
$f(\mathbf{s}) = c_0 + \sum_{j=1}^N c_j\, s_j + \sum_{1 \le j < k \le N}
c_{jk}\, s_j s_k = \mathbf{c}^\top \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{s})$.

\paragraph{Non-negativity certificate ($p_0$).}
The polynomial $p_0(\mathbf{s}) = f(\mathbf{s})$ has coefficients
$\alpha_0 = c_0$, $\alpha_j = c_j$, $\alpha_{jk} = c_{jk}$.
The certificate matrix is:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:A0}
  A_0 \in \R^{N \times N}, \qquad
  (A_0)_{j,j} = c_j, \qquad
  (A_0)_{j,k} = \tfrac{1}{2} c_{jk} \;\;(j \ne k).
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Leak-detection certificate ($p_i$, $i \in \calU$).}
The polynomial $p_i(\mathbf{s}) = f(\mathbf{s}) - s_i$ has coefficients
$\alpha_0 = c_0$, $\alpha_j = c_j - \delta_{ij}$, $\alpha_{jk} = c_{jk}$.
The certificate matrix is:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Ai}
  A_i \in \R^{N \times N}, \qquad
  (A_i)_{j,j} = c_j - \delta_{ij}, \qquad
  (A_i)_{j,k} = \tfrac{1}{2} c_{jk} \;\;(j \ne k).
\end{equation}
In other words, $A_i = A_0 - \mathbf{e}_i \mathbf{e}_i^\top$, where
$\mathbf{e}_i$ is the $i$-th standard basis vector.

\paragraph{Complete spectral SDP.}
Substituting~\eqref{eq:A0}--\eqref{eq:Ai} into
\Cref{prop:spectral-cert} and optimizing over $\mathbf{c}$:

\begin{equation}\label{eq:spectral-sdp}
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
  R^* \le \bar{R}_\text{spec} = \min_{\mathbf{c}} \quad
  & \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{b} \\[3pt]
  \text{s.t.} \quad
  & c_0 \ge 0 \\
  & A_0(\mathbf{c}) \succeq 0
    & & \text{(non-negativity)} \\
  & A_0(\mathbf{c}) - \mathbf{e}_i \mathbf{e}_i^\top \succeq 0
    \quad \forall i \in \calU
    & & \text{(leak detection)}
\end{aligned}
}
\end{equation}

\begin{proposition}[Complexity]\label{prop:spectral-complexity}
The spectral SDP~\eqref{eq:spectral-sdp} has:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $d = 1 + N + \binom{N}{2}$ variables (the entries of $\mathbf{c}$),
  \item $|\calU| + 1$ PSD cones of size~$N$,
  \item 1 scalar inequality ($c_0 \ge 0$).
\end{itemize}
Compare with the full SOS SDP~\eqref{eq:sdp-tight}, which has
$d + (|\calU|+1) \cdot \binom{N+1}{2}$ variables and
$|\calU|+1$ PSD cones of size $N+1$.

The spectral formulation
eliminates all $\binom{N+1}{2}$ auxiliary Gram-matrix entries
per constraint, since the Gram matrix $Q$ is fully determined by
$\mathbf{c}$ via~\eqref{eq:block-gram}.
\end{proposition}

\paragraph{Eigenvalue-based feasibility check.}
For a {\bfseries fixed} candidate $\mathbf{c}$ (e.g., obtained from a
previous iteration or a heuristic), all constraints
in~\eqref{eq:spectral-sdp} can be checked by computing:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item $c_0 \ge 0$ (scalar comparison),
  \item $\lambda_{\min}(A_0)$ via Lanczos
        ($A_0$ is $N \times N$, cost $O(N^2 k)$),
  \item $\lambda_{\min}(A_0 - \mathbf{e}_i \mathbf{e}_i^\top)$
        for each $i \in \calU$ (rank-1 downdate of $A_0$, each
        $O(N^2 k)$ or cheaper via Cauchy interlacing).
\end{enumerate}
Total: $O(|\calU| \cdot N^2 k)$ where $k$ is the number of Lanczos
iterations.

\begin{proposition}[Bound Ordering]
\label{prop:spectral-ordering}
$R^* \le \bar{R}_\text{SOS} \le \bar{R}_\text{spec}$.
The spectral bound is a valid (but potentially looser) upper bound
on the leakage probability. If $\bar{R}_\text{spec} \le \varepsilon$,
then $\Prob_\mu(\textsc{Leak}) \le \varepsilon$ for all $\mu \in \calP$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
The feasible set of~\eqref{eq:spectral-sdp} is a subset of the feasible
set of~\eqref{eq:sdp-tight}: any $\mathbf{c}$ feasible for the spectral
SDP induces a specific $Q \succeq 0$ (via the canonical
choice~\eqref{eq:block-gram}) that is feasible for the SOS SDP.
The converse may fail because the SOS SDP can use different diagonal
entries. Since the feasible set is smaller, the minimum of
$\mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{b}$ is at least as large:
$\bar{R}_\text{spec} \ge \bar{R}_\text{SOS} \ge R^*$.
\end{proof}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{llll}
\toprule
\textbf{Method}
  & \textbf{Per-command cost}
  & \textbf{Adaptive?}
  & \textbf{Bound quality} \\
\midrule
Spectral relaxation~\eqref{eq:spectral-sdp}
  & $O(|\calU| \cdot N^2 k)$ eigenvalues
  & Yes
  & Upper bound (conservative) \\
SOS SDP~\eqref{eq:sdp-tight}
  & $O(|\calU| \cdot N^{3.5})$ interior-point
  & Yes
  & Upper bound (tightest) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\begin{remark}[Growing File Sets]
When $N$ is unknown or changes at runtime,
the matrix $A_0(\mathbf{c})$ is assembled from the current
coefficient vector and transition matrices.
No offline precomputation is invalidated when $N$ changes.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Hierarchy~\cite{moreno2025}]
The canonical choice $Q_{j,j} = \alpha_j$ is the simplest spectral
certificate. Tighter bounds can be obtained by optimizing
the free diagonal entries $Q_{j,j}$, which adds $N$ scalar variables
per constraint but keeps the cones at size~$N$. In the extreme,
optimizing all entries of $Q$ recovers the full SOS
SDP~\eqref{eq:sdp-tight}. The spectral framework
of~\cite{moreno2025} provides a principled hierarchy interpolating
between these extremes.
\end{remark}

\begin{thebibliography}{9}
\bibitem{lasserre2001} J.~B.~Lasserre, ``Global optimization with
  polynomials and the problem of moments,'' \emph{SIAM J.\ Optim.},
  11(3):796--817, 2001.
\bibitem{sherali1990} H.~D.~Sherali and W.~P.~Adams, ``A hierarchy of
  relaxations \ldots for zero-one programming,'' \emph{SIAM J.\ Discrete
  Math.}, 3(3):411--430, 1990.
\bibitem{prajna2004} S.~Prajna and A.~Jadbabaie, ``Safety verification of
  hybrid systems using barrier certificates,'' \emph{HSCC}, 2004.
\bibitem{wiesemann2014} W.~Wiesemann, D.~Kuhn, M.~Sim,
  ``Distributionally robust convex optimization,''
  \emph{Operations Research}, 62(6):1358--1376, 2014.
\bibitem{moreno2025} E.~Moreno and V.~Chandrasekaran, ``Spectral
  methods for polynomial optimization,'' \emph{arXiv:2507.16272}, 2025.
\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}
